skill.name: convex-setup-auth
skill.description: Sets up Convex authentication with user management, identity mapping, and access control. Use this skill when adding login or signup to a Convex app, configuring Convex Auth, Clerk, WorkOS AuthKit, Auth0, or custom JWT providers, wiring auth.config.ts, protecting queries and mutations with ctx.auth.getUserIdentity(), creating a users table with identity mapping, or setting up role-based access control, even if the user just says 'add auth' or 'make it require login.'
skill.summary: Sets up Convex authentication with user management, identity mapping, and access control for multiple providers.
skill.author: get-convex

security_audit.summary: This skill is a documentation and reference guide for setting up Convex authentication. All 254 static findings are false positives triggered by legitimate documentation patterns (markdown code blocks, environment variable names in docs, documentation URLs, and references to external commands). The skill does not contain executable malicious code. It provides guidance for configuring auth providers which requires reading documentation and writing configuration files. Risk level set to low due to documentation-only nature with no actual code execution capability.

low_findings.title: Documentation Pattern - Shell Command References
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged markdown code blocks containing shell commands (npx convex dev, npm install, etc.) as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution'. These are legitimate documentation code blocks in markdown files, not actual command execution. The skill is a reference guide containing instructions, not executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Markdown backticks (```) are used for code formatting in documentation. The detected 'backtick execution' patterns are in code blocks within markdown reference files, not actual shell execution calls.

low_findings.title: Documentation Pattern - Hardcoded URLs
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged URLs in documentation as 'Hardcoded URLs'. These are legitimate links to official Convex documentation, Auth0 docs, Clerk docs, and WorkOS docs. No credential exfiltration or malicious network behavior exists.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: These URLs point to official documentation for Convex and its supported auth providers. They are documentation references, not network exfiltration points.

low_findings.title: Documentation Pattern - Environment Variable Names
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged environment variable names in documentation (CLERK_SECRET_KEY, AUTH0_CLIENT_ID, etc.) as 'Generic API/secret keys'. These are legitimate documentation of expected environment variables for auth configuration, not actual secret values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Environment variable names in documentation are not secrets. The skill instructs users to set their own values, it does not contain or expose actual credentials.

low_findings.title: Documentation Pattern - Weak Crypto Heuristic
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged documentation containing 'crypto' keywords as 'Weak cryptographic algorithm'. The detections are in documentation URLs and descriptions, not actual cryptographic implementations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The 'crypto' keyword appears in documentation URLs (crypto.dev, cryptographic) and skill descriptions about authentication. No actual cryptographic algorithms are implemented in this skill.

low_findings.title: Documentation Pattern - Database File Reference
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 'SQLite database file' mentions in documentation. These references describe Convex Auth's internal auth tables, not actual SQLite file access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Convex Auth uses auth tables (not SQLite directly) for user management. The references are documentation about Convex's auth table structure.

content.user_title: Set up Convex authentication with multiple providers
content.value_statement: Adding authentication to a Convex app requires understanding multiple providers, environment configuration, and security patterns. This skill guides you through setting up Convex Auth, Clerk, Auth0, or WorkOS with proper user management and access control.
actual_capabilities: Guide users through Convex authentication setup with multiple providers (Convex Auth, Clerk, Auth0, WorkOS)
Configure auth.config.ts for provider-specific JWT validation
Set up user management with identity mapping and users table
Protect backend queries and mutations with ctx.auth.getUserIdentity()
Implement role-based access control and authorization patterns
Configure environment variables for development and production
limitations: Does not execute browser automation to validate login flows automatically
Cannot complete interactive CLI steps (Auth0 login, Convex deployment setup) on behalf of the user
Does not provide pre-generated secret keys; users must obtain their own from providers

use_cases.title: Add authentication to a new Convex app
use_cases.description: Set up user login for a fresh Convex project by choosing an auth provider, configuring the backend auth config, wiring the frontend provider, and adding protected queries.
use_cases.target_user: Developers starting a new Convex project who need user authentication

use_cases.title: Integrate existing auth provider with Convex
use_cases.description: Connect an existing Clerk, Auth0, or WorkOS account to a Convex backend by configuring token validation, setting up environment variables, and wiring the client provider.
use_cases.target_user: Developers with existing auth provider accounts who want to add Convex backend

use_cases.title: Add role-based access control to protected endpoints
use_cases.description: Extend existing Convex authentication by adding authorization checks, ownership validation, and role-based access control to queries and mutations.
use_cases.target_user: Developers who need fine-grained permissions beyond basic authentication

prompt_templates.title: Basic auth setup request
prompt_templates.prompt: Add authentication to my Convex app. I want users to be able to sign up and sign in.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants basic authentication without specifying a provider

prompt_templates.title: Specific provider request
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up Clerk authentication for my Convex app. I already have a Clerk account and want to integrate it.
prompt_templates.scenario: User has an existing auth provider they want to use

prompt_templates.title: Protected backend request
prompt_templates.prompt: Protect my Convex queries and mutations so only authenticated users can access them. Use ctx.auth.getUserIdentity() for the auth checks.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to secure existing backend functions

prompt_templates.title: Production-ready setup request
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up authentication for my Convex app with production-ready configuration. Include dev and production environment variables, proper redirect URLs, and full access control.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants complete production authentication setup

output_examples.input: Add authentication to my Convex app with Clerk
output_examples.output:
I'll help you set up Clerk authentication with Convex. First, let me confirm a few things:

1. Do you have a Clerk account and application created?
2. Which frontend framework are you using (React, Next.js, Vite)?
3. Do you want local-only setup or production-ready configuration?

If you have Clerk set up already, I'll need:
- Your Clerk Frontend API URL
- Your Clerk publishable key
- To activate the Convex integration in Clerk dashboard

output_examples.input: Protect my queries so only logged-in users can access them
Set up user management with identity mapping

best_practices: Always verify identity server-side using ctx.auth.getUserIdentity() instead of trusting client-provided user IDs
Keep dev and production environment variables separate; never use dev credentials in production
Ask the user which auth provider they want before writing setup code; do not assume
anti_patterns: Do not skip the auth provider quickstart if the app is starting from scratch
Do not assume local Auth0/Clerk/WorkOS settings match production; verify production configuration separately
Do not invent auth patterns from memory; use official Convex documentation as the source of truth

faq.question: Which authentication providers does this skill support?
faq.answer: The skill supports Convex Auth (built-in), Clerk, Auth0, WorkOS AuthKit, and custom JWT providers. Ask the user which provider they want before proceeding.

faq.question: Does this skill handle secret keys and credentials?
faq.answer: No. The skill guides users on what environment variables to set, but users must obtain their own API keys and credentials from their chosen auth provider.

faq.question: Can this skill complete the full authentication setup automatically?
faq.answer: Partially. The skill can write configuration files and provide instructions, but interactive CLI steps (like Auth0 CLI login or Convex deployment setup) require the user to complete those steps manually.

faq.question: How does this skill handle production vs development setup?
faq.answer: The skill asks users whether they want local-only or production-ready setup. For production, it ensures production credentials, redirect URLs, and deployment configuration are all covered.

faq.question: Does this skill add a users table automatically?
faq.answer: Only when the documentation and app requirements call for it. For Convex Auth, the skill uses built-in auth tables. For third-party providers, it adds app-level user storage only if needed.

faq.question: How does this skill validate that authentication works?
faq.answer: The skill offers to verify that ctx.auth.getUserIdentity() returns a valid identity in protected backend functions after login. If browser automation is available, it can test the full sign-in flow.
